findings.summary: Settings class accumulates 30+ attributes across OCR, classification, daemon, image, and logging concerns in a single flat class with no grouping
evidence: Settings.__init__ calls 7 private _load_* methods, each setting a disjoint group of attributes (API, LLM, tags, daemon, image, logging, classification)

Classification-specific settings (CLASSIFY_PERSON_FIELD_ID, CLASSIFY_MAX_CHARS, CLASSIFY_MAX_TOKENS, etc.) are mixed with OCR-specific settings (OCR_DPI, OCR_MAX_SIDE, PAGE_WORKERS) in the same object

Every consumer receives the full Settings object even when it only needs a subset (e.g. OcrProvider only needs AI_MODELS, OCR_MAX_SIDE, OCR_REFUSAL_MARKERS, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, MAX_RETRIES, MAX_RETRY_BACKOFF_SECONDS)
findings.suggestion: Group related settings into nested dataclasses or NamedTuples (e.g. LLMSettings, TagSettings, ClassifySettings) and pass only the relevant group to consumers.

findings.summary: ClassificationProcessor.process() method handles 9 distinct steps including refresh, error check, claim, content check, refusal check, truncation, LLM call, validation, and metadata application in a single method body
evidence: Lines 66-152: process() method is ~86 lines with 5 levels of conditional branching and a try/finally block

The method checks error tag presence, claims processing tag, checks empty content, checks refusal markers, truncates content, calls classifier, checks empty result, checks generic document type, and applies classification -- each a distinct responsibility
The same pattern is repeated in OcrProcessor.process() (src/ocr/worker.py lines 50-135) with similarly mixed responsibilities
findings.suggestion: Extract the sequential steps into a pipeline or break the method into a high-level orchestrator that calls clearly named step methods, reducing the cognitive load of the single process() body.

findings.summary: _OpenAIClientHolder class wraps a single optional reference with init/get/is_ready methods, then three module-level functions forward to it, creating a double layer of indirection for a module-global singleton
evidence: Lines 21-43: _OpenAIClientHolder class has 3 methods (init, is_ready, get) that each do one trivial operation on self._client
Lines 46-55: Three module-level functions (init_openai_client, get_openai_client, is_openai_client_ready) each forward a single call to _openai_holder with no added logic
The docstring says 'Avoids a bare module-level mutable' but the holder is itself a bare module-level mutable instance (_openai_holder = _OpenAIClientHolder())
findings.suggestion: Replace the class + forwarding functions with a simple module-level variable and direct accessor functions, or use the holder class directly without the forwarding layer.

findings.summary: _TaxonomyKind NamedTuple bundles 6 fields to pass between _get_or_create_item_id and per-kind factory methods, but it is only constructed in two places and consumed in one
evidence: Lines 107-115: _TaxonomyKind has 6 fields (items, mapping, normalizer, allow_substring, creator, label)
Lines 167-177: _correspondent_kind() and _document_type_kind() exist solely to construct _TaxonomyKind instances

Lines 179-208: _get_or_create_item_id receives a Callable[[], _TaxonomyKind] factory and immediately calls it, then accesses all 6 fields -- the factory indirection exists only because the method calls self.refresh() on failure which may change the underlying data
get_or_create_tag_ids (lines 216-247) does not use _TaxonomyKind at all, duplicating the match-create-refresh pattern inline
findings.suggestion: Consider whether the NamedTuple is earning its keep; the tag path duplicates the pattern without it, suggesting the abstraction could be simplified or the tag path should reuse it.

findings.summary: classifier/__init__.py and ocr/__init__.py define __all__ with re-exports, while common/__init__.py has only a docstring with no exports
evidence: classifier/__init__.py imports and re-exports 5 symbols via __all__
ocr/__init__.py imports and re-exports 5 symbols via __all__
common/__init__.py contains only a single docstring line with no imports or __all__
All actual imports from common use direct submodule paths (e.g. from common.config import Settings, from common.paperless import PaperlessClient)
findings.suggestion: Either add re-exports to common/__init__.py for its primary public API (Settings, PaperlessClient) or remove the re-exports from the other packages so all three follow the same convention.

findings.summary: ocr/daemon.py main() and helper functions lack docstrings while classifier/daemon.py sibling functions have them
evidence: classifier/daemon.py: _process_document has docstring 'Process a single Paperless document...', _iter_docs_to_classify has docstring 'Yield documents that should be classified.', main() has multi-line docstring
ocr/daemon.py: _process_document has docstring but _iter_docs_to_ocr has no docstring, main() has no docstring
findings.suggestion: Add matching docstrings to ocr/daemon.py's _iter_docs_to_ocr and main() for consistency with the classifier sibling.

findings.summary: daemon_loop.py and ocr/worker.py use bare 'except Exception' catches that swallow all errors with only logging, while sibling code uses narrowly typed exception tuples
evidence: daemon_loop.py line 42: 'except Exception:' catches any exception from a work item and logs it -- this is intentional fault isolation but contrasts with the specific PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS used elsewhere
ocr/worker.py line 162: 'except Exception:' in _ocr_pages_in_parallel catches any OCR page failure broadly
daemon_loop.py line 137: _safe_item_summary uses 'except Exception:' to prevent logging from crashing
Meanwhile tags.py, claims.py, stale_lock.py, preflight.py all catch the specific PAPERLESS_CALL_EXCEPTIONS tuple for the same kind of external-call error handling
findings.suggestion: The thread-pool fault-isolation catches are defensible, but document them with comments explaining why broad catches are used in those specific locations to distinguish from the pattern of specific exception tuples used everywhere else.

findings.summary: PaperlessClient has two different raise_for_status patterns: _raise_for_status_if_server_error (only 5xx) in _get/_patch/_post, and direct response.raise_for_status() (all 4xx+) in get_document, _list_all, update_document, update_document_metadata, download_content
evidence: Lines 48-51: _raise_for_status_if_server_error only raises for status_code >= 500, used inside retry-decorated methods
Lines 76, 137, 148, 160, 199: plain response.raise_for_status() is called AFTER the retry-wrapped _get/_patch returns, catching all non-2xx codes

This means 4xx errors from the low-level methods are silently returned (no raise), then caught by the caller's raise_for_status -- the two-phase pattern is not documented and creates confusion about which layer handles which error class
findings.suggestion: Document the two-phase pattern in the class docstring or consolidate to a single raise pattern. The intent appears to be 'retry on 5xx, fail fast on 4xx' but this is implicit.

findings.summary: classifier/prompts.py and ocr/prompts.py contain the LLM system prompts and the JSON schema definition but have no dedicated tests validating schema correctness or prompt contract invariants
evidence: No test file exists at tests/unit/classifier/test_prompts.py or tests/unit/ocr/test_prompts.py

CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA in classifier/prompts.py defines 'strict': True and 'additionalProperties': False -- if field names drift from parse_classification_response's expected keys, no test would catch it
The 7 required fields in CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA must match the 7 fields in ClassificationResult dataclass, but this mapping is untested
findings.suggestion: Add a schema-contract test that verifies CLASSIFICATION_JSON_SCHEMA's required fields match ClassificationResult's fields, preventing silent drift.

findings.summary: Worker tests use extensive @patch decorators on module-level imports, coupling tests to the internal import paths of the worker modules rather than to behavior
evidence: test_worker.py (classifier): 23 out of 24 test methods use @patch('classifier.worker.claim_processing_tag') and @patch('classifier.worker.release_processing_tag')

test_worker.py (ocr): most tests use @patch('ocr.worker.release_processing_tag'), @patch('ocr.worker.claim_processing_tag'), @patch('ocr.worker.bytes_to_images'), and @patch('ocr.worker.assemble_full_text')
Any refactor that moves claim_processing_tag to a different import path or restructures the worker module would break all these tests without changing behavior
findings.suggestion: Inject dependencies (claim function, release function) via constructor or method parameters to reduce reliance on module-path patching.

findings.summary: Two files contain 'raise AssertionError("unreachable")' guards after for-loops that provably always return or raise, with pragma: no cover -- a defensive overengineering pattern typical of LLM-generated code
evidence: paperless.py line 122: 'raise AssertionError(...)  # pragma: no cover' after a for loop over candidates where the last iteration always re-raises
retry.py line 55: 'raise AssertionError("unreachable")  # pragma: no cover' after a for loop that always returns or raises
findings.suggestion: Remove these unreachable guards or replace with a simple comment. Type checkers can be satisfied with typing.assert_never or by restructuring the return.

findings.summary: Multiple trivial one-liner or two-liner functions have multi-line docstrings that restate what the code does without adding insight
evidence: shutdown.py: request_shutdown, is_shutdown_requested, reset_shutdown are one-line functions; register_signal_handlers has a docstring 'Install SIGTERM/SIGINT handlers. Must be called from the main thread.' -- the 'Must be called from main thread' is useful but the rest is obvious

llm.py lines 46-51: init_openai_client and get_openai_client are trivial forwarding functions with no docstrings, but the class they forward to (_OpenAIClientHolder) has a multi-line docstring explaining it 'Avoids a bare module-level mutable' when it IS a bare module-level mutable

normalizers.py: normalize_simple and normalize_name have docstrings with >>> examples that are good, but the docstring text restates the implementation ('Collapse whitespace and lowercase a string' for a function that does ' '.join(value.lower().split()))
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on trivial functions to single-line summaries or remove entirely when the function name is self-documenting. Keep doctest examples as they serve as executable documentation.

findings.summary: Entry/exit logging and info-level logging on every step creates noisy output for normal operations, a pattern common in LLM-generated code
evidence: paperless.py lines 152-161: update_document logs both 'Updating document' and 'Successfully updated document' for every call -- the success log adds no information beyond the absence of an error
paperless.py lines 197-200: update_document_metadata similarly logs before and after
retry.py lines 65-72: _sleep_backoff logs every sleep with 5 structured fields (delay, attempt, max_retries, max_backoff) at INFO level
classifier/worker.py line 81: log.info('Classifying document') and line 305: log.info('Document classification applied') bracket every document
findings.suggestion: Consolidate entry/exit log pairs into a single completion log at DEBUG level, and reduce _sleep_backoff logging to DEBUG. Keep error and warning logs at their current levels.
